plan.contextChunk:
Simply placing them at evenly spaced intervals along the axis in the
image won't be correct. Due to geometric distortion introduced by
perspective projection, the tick marks are not evenly spaced within the
image plane. This distortion affects each axis in a distinct manner.

Let's start assuming we know the location of some arbitrary 3D distance
to the origin on each axis that we will denote by $\alpha_X$, $\alpha_Y$
and $\alpha_Z$. The question is, where do we place the ticks for
$k\alpha_X$, $k\alpha_Y$ and $k\alpha_Z$ for all $k$? We will use the
cross-ratio to calibrate the projection of the world coordinate system
in the image plane. For doing this we will need the location of the
horizon line of a reference plane (i.e. the ground plane), the position
of the orthogonal vanishing point and the real measure of one object in
the scene.

As shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a) we can use the cross-ratio
to estimate the location of the points $Y= k \alpha_X$ where $\alpha_X$
is an arbitrary constant as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (b),
and the corresponding image location $r$, for $k=1$, is chosen
arbitrarily as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a). Due to
perspective projection, the image projection of the point
$Y= k \alpha_X$ will not be evenly spaced in the image. In this
particular example we chose the orientation of the world axis in image
coordinates to be aligned with the location of the vanishing points.

![Using the cross-ratio invariant to project the world coordinates into the image using only the vanishing points.](figures/single_view_3d/axis_calibration_procedure.png){width="100%" #fig-axis_calibration_procedure}

In order to calibrate the axis we need to know the dimensions of an
object in the scene. We need measurements along the three axis. In this
example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the
table base (50 cm). Using these reference measurements we can solve for
the image locations of the ticks $\alpha_X=\alpha_Y=\alpha_Z=1$ m.

@fig-office_all_calibrated_box shows the final calibrated axes with
ticks placed each 50 cm along each of the three axes.

![Calibrated world axes. Each axis is calibrated using the measurement of a scene element parallel to the world-coordinate axis. In this example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the table base (50 cm). From the calibrated axes, you can see that the office is around 250 cm wide.](figures/single_view_3d/office_all_calibrated_box.png){width="100%" #fig-office_all_calibrated_box}

From this calibrated system we can directly read the height of the
camera. The location of the camera coincides with the location of the
horizon line because, in this example, the ground plane is horizontal.

elements: World coordinate system origin O
X_W axis (blue arrow)
Y_W axis (red arrow)
Z_W axis (green arrow)
Points P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 along Y_W
Image plane (tilted rectangle)
Projection lines from P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 to image plane
Vanishing points v1 (blue), v2 (green), v3 (red) in image plane
Projected axes (colored lines) in image plane
Ticks on image of each axis (5 for each axis)
Labels for axes (X_W, Y_W, Z_W)
Labels for projected tick distances (α_x, 2α_x, ..., 5α_x and similarly for y and z)
Horizon line/orthogonal vanishing points
Reference measurements (annotated arrows showing distances α_x, α_y, α_z)

interactions.label: Show projection lines
interactions.teaches: Toggle to reveal or hide how 3D points are mapped onto the 2D image plane via perspective projection.

interactions.label: Reference axis (X/Y/Z)
interactions.teaches: Select which world axis (X, Y, or Z) is being calibrated to see how its ticks project onto the image.

interactions.label: Real-world tick distance (cm)
interactions.teaches: Sets the real-world spacing between ticks for calibration, demonstrating how this measurement controls the projected tick intervals on each axis.

interactions.label: Show horizon line
interactions.teaches: Reveal the horizon line, which aligns with the camera height and is crucial for geometric calibration using vanishing points.

demo_steps.title: Perspective Projection Setup
demo_steps.narration: Let's begin by visualizing the full 3D scene. The world coordinate axes are displayed with colored arrows, and the projection lines show how points in 3D space are mapped onto the tilted image plane. Notice how each world point projects along a dashed line to its corresponding spot in the image.

demo_steps.title: Choosing a Reference Axis
demo_steps.narration: Select the axis you want to calibrate—in this case, the X axis. Observe the world axis and its projection onto the image. Changing the reference axis updates which ticks and projections are highlighted, illustrating how each direction is affected differently by perspective.

demo_steps.title: Adjust Real-World Tick Distance
demo_steps.narration: Now let's change the real-world tick distance to 100 cm. Notice how the spacing between the ticks in the image shifts dramatically. Perspective causes the projected tick marks to bunch up or spread out, depending on their distance along the axis relative to the camera.

demo_steps.title: Mapping All Axes
demo_steps.narration: Cycle through each axis using the control. You'll see that each axis projects differently, causing its ticks to have unique non-uniform spacing. This highlights why even spacing in world coordinates leads to distorted placements in the image, and why calibration must consider perspective for each direction.

demo_steps.title: Reveal Horizon and Vanishing Points
demo_steps.narration: Turn on the horizon line to see its alignment with the vanishing points of the axis projections. This line corresponds to the camera height. Using the horizon, vanishing points, and a known scene measurement, you can calibrate the entire coordinate system accurately—even from a single image.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial view should be a slightly oblique 3D angle showing both the world axes, the image plane, and vanishing points clearly. Allow user to zoom and pan for detail.
interactionPlan.notes: Accurate dashed-line projection from 3D points to the image plane is essential. Each axis' tick marks must be projected using perspective and the cross-ratio, not linear subdivision. Render vanishing points far enough to see horizon intersections even if off image. Consider using transparency for the image plane so both world and image spaces are visible simultaneously.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The rendered 3D axes are present but the perspective is not as dramatic/tilted as in the source, making the image plane overlap less obviously with the world axes.
The vanishing points are shown as large colored dots, but their arrangement doesn't quite match the labeled positions in the source (e.g., v2, v3 swapped).
Measurement labels and alpha ticks are over-cluttered and less visually clear than in the original figure.
Projection lines for P2-P4 are present, but the perspective lines from P1 and the camera center are not as clear as in (a).
UI controls, step demo, and horizon line are present but not part of the original 2D figure, potentially cluttering the scene.
evaluation.notes: Strong interactive demo accurately depicts most world/image plane geometry but label clarity and camera/plane orientation differ from the original.
action_items: Adjust the orientation of the image plane and axes to more strongly match the dramatic perspective and apparent foreshortening in the source figure.
Refine vanishing point order and placement to exactly match v1, v2, v3 from the original, and ensure their label coloring/placement is clear and distinct.
Simplify, declutter, and enlarge labels and alpha annotations for clarity and closer readability to the source.
Review the tick mark and axis segment positions to strictly follow the stylings and arrangements in both panels of the reference image.
Consider camera and plane rendering so that the spatial context (camera center, eye rays) matches the articulated geometry from the source.

feedback.rationale: The primary issues are with rendering accuracy, label clarity, and spatial arrangement rather than fundamental conceptual or planning errors.
action_items: Adjust the orientation of the image plane and axes to more strongly match the dramatic perspective and apparent foreshortening in the source figure.

Review the tick mark and axis segment positions to strictly follow the stylings and arrangements in both panels of the reference image.
Consider camera and plane rendering so that the spatial context (camera center, eye rays) matches the articulated geometry from the source.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The world and image coordinate systems are overlapping in 3D space, making separation unclear compared to the clear split in the source figure.
The image panel (right 2D projection) in the screenshot is rendered in 3D and is shown at an angle, unlike the flat, head-on 2D representation in the textbook.
Text labels overlap and are cluttered in many places, especially for axes and tick marks.
Typeface and weight of labels do not closely match the distinct bold serif and italic styles in the source figure.
Some colored arrows (axes) and vanishing points marking (colored dots) are not as visually distinct or well-placed as in the textbook, causing some confusion.
evaluation.notes: All core geometric elements are present with rich interactions, but spatial layout and label placement/clarity diverge notably from the textbook illustration.
action_items: Separate the 'world coordinates' 3D diagram (left) and the 'image' 2D panel (right) spatially as in the reference; do not overlap or tilt the right-hand panel in 3D.
Improve label placement to avoid overlap and mimic the bold/italic/serif styles and sizing from the textbook more closely.
Flatten the right-side panel (the image projection) so that it appears as a true 2D view and not in 3D perspective.
Adjust axes lengths and font scaling to better match proportions in the reference, reducing clutter.
Tweak the camera and initial view so that all axes and vanishing points are properly visible without overlap.

feedback.rationale: The issues center on spatial layout, label styling, and visual clarity—indicating the plan is largely correct, but the generated figure needs improvements in execution and styling.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The black world coordinate dots (P1, P2, P3, P4) are present but the rightmost one is slightly misaligned to the diagram in figure (a).
The 'alpha' tick marks and their labels are present but are slightly more visually cluttered than in the original.
The axes colors and directions match closely, but the X_W and Z_W arrows in figure (a) are less prominent in the generated output.
